title: Paris-Brest
subtitle: Choux ring filled with praline cream and flaked almonds
excerpt: A large ring of choux pastry, split and filled with a light praline cream made from whipped cream, ground almonds, and coffee. Topped with flaked almonds and icing sugar. Created in 1910 to celebrate the Paris-Brest bicycle race; the wheel shape is the whole point.
sourceNotes: Paris-Brest was created in 1910 by patissier Louis Durand of Maisons-Laffitte to celebrate the Paris-Brest-Paris bicycle race, and is documented in French culinary history and in mid-twentieth-century French pastry manuals. The choux ring filled with praline cream in the shape of a bicycle wheel is fully public domain and widely documented across French baking literature.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Bake the choux ring up to four hours ahead. Make and chill the filling. Assemble within two hours of serving.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: fan oven; conventional 220°C. Do not open the oven door for the first 20 minutes.

glossaryTerms.definition: A cooked paste of butter, water, flour, and eggs that puffs dramatically in the oven as steam from the high water content creates a hollow interior. Used for éclairs, profiteroles, and chouquettes.
A lightened pastry cream made by folding whipped cream or additional butter into a chilled crème pâtissière. Lighter and more stable than plain whipped cream.

content.text: Paris-Brest is a party pastry: a large
ring, baked until deeply golden and hollow, split in half, and filled generously with a

flavoured with ground almonds and coffee. The filling here uses a simple base of whipped cream and pastry cream rather than full praline paste, which produces a lighter result with less equipment and similar flavour.

The ring is piped freehand onto a lined tray (draw a 22 cm circle as a guide if needed), then covered with flaked almonds and baked. The choux must be very thoroughly baked: pale or soft shells collapse when filled. The base should feel completely dry and hollow when lifted and tapped.

attrs.prepNote: diced, for the choux; plus 50 g softened, for the filling cream
for the choux; plus 20 g for the pastry cream base
large; 4 for the choux, 1 beaten for the egg wash
to press onto the ring before baking
cold, for whipping into the filling
dissolved in 1 tsp of hot water, for the filling

content.text: Make and bake the choux ring

Heat the oven to 200°C fan (220°C conventional). Draw a 22 cm circle on a sheet of baking paper and flip it pencil-side down onto a baking tray. Bring {{water}}, the choux-quantity {{unsalted-butter}}, and {{sea-salt-fine}} to a full boil in a saucepan. Remove from the heat, add the choux-quantity {{plain-flour}} all at once, and beat hard until a smooth dough forms and pulls away from the pan sides. Return to medium heat and stir for 1 minute to dry. Cool for 5 minutes. Beat in 4 of the {{eggs}} one at a time until the paste is smooth and glossy; it should fall from the spoon in a slow V. Transfer to a piping bag fitted with a large round nozzle. Pipe two adjacent rings around the drawn circle, then a third ring on top in the centre of the join. Brush with beaten egg and press {{flaked-almonds}} onto the surface. Bake for 32–35 minutes until deeply golden and completely firm and hollow when tapped on the base. Do not open the oven door for the first 20 minutes. Cool completely on a rack.

Whisk {{egg-yolks}}, {{caster-sugar}}, and the pastry cream-quantity {{plain-flour}} until smooth. Bring {{whole-milk}} to a simmer, pour over the egg mixture, and return to the pan. Cook over medium heat, stirring constantly, until thick and bubbling. Stir in the filling-quantity softened {{unsalted-butter}}, {{ground-almonds}}, and the dissolved {{instant-coffee}}. Press cling film onto the surface and refrigerate until cold. Whip {{double-cream}} to soft peaks. Fold one-third of the whipped cream into the cold pastry cream to loosen it, then fold in the remainder. Split the cooled choux ring horizontally with a serrated knife. Pipe or spoon the filling generously into the base half, then replace the lid. Dust with {{icing-sugar}} and refrigerate for 30 minutes before slicing.
The choux ring must be completely dry inside before filling. If uncertain, bake for 5 more minutes: an underbaked ring will collapse when split.

For a traditional praline version: make a dry caramel with 100 g caster sugar, add 100 g toasted whole hazelnuts, pour onto a lined tray, and blitz to a rough paste when set. Stir into the pastry cream in place of the ground almonds.
Individual Paris-Brest can be piped as small rings of about 8 cm diameter. Reduce the baking time by 5–8 minutes.
